speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of the interview of Adam Melcer, M-E-L-C-E-R, on November 29th, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Do you remember anything specific about the treatment of children or young people who were in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camps, there were no children. I didn't see no children in camps because, as I said, with segregation when they do the [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] right and left, the children were taken away. So only people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] could go to work were selected to stay maybe alive. So children were never in the camp. Maybe in some places. I don't know. Not my places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any special actions in the camp? Any special roundups or punishments in the camp [INAUDIBLE]? Any special kinds of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --events that they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any religious observances in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some groups maybe did some services.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how religious leaders were treated there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing like everybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any activities that the people in the barracks formed that were cultural or religious in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe there were some groups that were talking and this and that, but nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any resistance in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sabotage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sabotage, possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any escapes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Escapes. People tried to escape. I remember somebody tried to escape from the camp. They brought him in and they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- any of you-- aware of events outside of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any famous visitors to the camps, such as the Red Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Jewish camps. Red Cross didn't show up for the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after Dachau? Or what happened-- with-- you went to Dachau on the-- on a-- on a march, and then what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there for a couple of weeks. And then, as I said, the American army came in and liberated on May 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, we-- All of a sudden we saw the American soldiers came in with the tanks, with the Jeeps, and the Germans didn't show up anymore. So they took over the camp and they gave us food. And some people were very hungry. Everybody was hungry, but some people ate too much the first time and got sick. And a lot of people died from the food, from the good food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you expect to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your special skills and talents helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helped, yeah. [? And the ?]-- But I would say only the question of luck. You cannot say. Nobody was smart. People who had money, didn't help. Could go with the money. It's just a question to be in the right place in the right time. It's a question of luck. There were no smart people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which army liberated you? Do you know which American Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 7th Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the General? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Patton.

speaker: interviewer
text: After your liberation, where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to the hospital. They took me to the hospital. Munich. I stayed about two, three weeks. And then I moved in with some friends of mine who lived in an apartment with the German people. It was in Pasing, was it? Pasing, near Munich. And then a little while later, I find out that-- yes, I remember that Eisenhower visited camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eisenhower visited us, and this was in already in Germany, later on. And on Yom Kippur, I came to-- I was-- that was after Poland already. After I came back from Poland. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's after-- in Poland I went in June. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June, I left with Donat and my other brother-in-law. We went to Poland [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I met my wife and the child. And he left-- he find his daughter there in Poland, my brother-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was alive also. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And Donat found his wife in Warsaw. She was liberated [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh we went-- I was in Poland 10 days. And I went back to Germany because we knew about in Poland-- I didn't want to stay in Poland. But from Poland to be unable to immigrate no place, so we left for Germany with the [INAUDIBLE] the whole group. My wife, my brother-in-law, and a woman who-- he was-- after his wife died, he was-- I mean, they wasn't married, but he lived with another woman. She took care of the baby, too. His girl. And she has a boy, which was also with Polish people. And all this group went back to Germany. I had some acquaintance in Poland-- a lawyer-- who gave us a paper. It was not official, but, you know, to travel, to be able to travel to Czechoslovakia [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Germany back. And with this paper, we have to be separated. My wife said she's going to Germany to work for her husband. And I said that I'm going to find my wife. And this is the way we went through and we went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any other surviving members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the reunion with your child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I remember. I was in Warsaw and the child, she recognized me because I was wearing glasses all the time. But she was more Catholic. She didn't know I'm Jewish or something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my wife was there. She came a couple months before me to the child. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I saw it's no way for me to stay in Poland, so we left and we went back to Germany and we landed in a DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feldafing. F-E-L-D-A-F-I-N-G. At Feldafing, there was a lot of Jewish-- only Jews DP camp. This was organized-- I don't know why-- by the Germans. But it was a DP camp just for DPs. There was maybe a couple thousand people there. And they gave us an apartment. As a matter of fact, we have a villa because we have the kind-- the baby-- she was that time four or five years old. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was a very nice-- as a matter of fact, it was a Jewish villa. It once belonged to Jews, but the Germans took it away. The military. It used to be a military camp, this Feldafing. But then there was no more German military camp. And we stayed there. This was about July, 1945 to January 19-- January-- or, December 1946. Meantime, they formed a Jewish newspaper in Munich organized by the joint-- American joint. So I was working there, and a couple of my friends from Feldafing which were printers, which I knew from Warsaw. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the doctor-- the editor-- was a guy from Vilna. [INAUDIBLE] was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr.--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the director of the newspaper. He was the editor of the paper. was the name of the paper, specifically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unserword. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] U-N-S-E-R-W-O-R-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our word. Our-- word. Word is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every word you say is a word. Everything you say is a word. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was there until we emigrated to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't get paid. All we got is food for the-- conserves, you know? This kind of food. American stuff. I didn't-- we got cigarettes, of course. People, I didn't-- I don't smoke, so I sold the cigarettes for something else. But we didn't get paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to America, did you have any assistance in getting here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had no assistance, but I came to America through the [INAUDIBLE]. But really, my travel was paid by my family in America. I got in touch with my family in America. They paid for our transfer, for the shipping, and they sent the affidavit really. So we didn't have-- we were not on the quota, I know. We were more-- to the family, we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came here, where did you move to first?

speaker: interviewee
text: I moved to my family. My uncle had a house in east New York and they gave us a room. And this way, we came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you add to your family once you arrived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have additional children when you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, later on. First of all, I stayed with my family about a half a year. I was working-- after 10 days in America, I got a job. And I got paid double the wages as an American makes at that time as a printer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you earning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How much were you earning?

speaker: interviewee
text: $75 A week. At that time, it was $40 on average. And I got-- the first minute-- I became a union member the first week. And they sent me to a place-- Shulsinger Brothers Publishing. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shulsinger. S-H-U-L-S-I-N-G-E-R. Shulsinger Brothers was a big printing in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. And I worked there for thirty years until I retired. And after my retirement, he closed up the shop because he was an Israeli. He went back to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other children in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My boy was born [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] February 6, '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin. And then another boy was born 10 years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does your present level of belief or observance compare with your pre-war level, or has that changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No change. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your children receive Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, my second boy when to Yeshiva and finished a high school Yeshiva too, and then went to college. And he is now a professor in college, in New Jersey. He has a degree of Doctor of Chemistry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My older boy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went to school and finished at Columbia University with a degree of Master's in Speech Therapy.

speaker: interviewer
text: To what extent do you think that the Holocaust changed you as a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I know what the world is, what you can expect from other people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: If--

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to-- you have to be a-- a [? mensch ?], that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had a message to give your children, what would you tell them about, as a result of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to expect too much from other people. You have to do what you can to make a living, a decent living, and be a decent person. That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you feel about the way you've lived your life and what's important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Family. And as I say, be a decent person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please identify the person in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam Melcer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in June, 1945, after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the person in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Adam Melcer after I came back from Poland to Germany in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Displaced Person camp. Was in 1940-- this picture is 1946. This was taken in Germany in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you please identify the person in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Alice, after we took her back from the Polish people. It was in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Alice, at graduation from high school in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is that about?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 16 years old. So 16-- 1955, isn't it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please identify the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Alice, and her husband, Robert, and her grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you please identify the person in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter, Suzanne. And this picture was taken in 1980. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [INTERPOSING VOICES] This is my great-grandchildren. These are the children of the grand-- I mean, she's the grandchildren of my daughter, Alice. The name is Taylor and Andrew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He's four years old. Four and six. Four and seven. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please identify the people in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is from the Polish family from Warsaw. These are the children of the family who took care of my daughter Alice during though war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Melcer, would you please identify the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Here's a picture of the son and the daughter of the people who took care of Alice during the war when they got-- when they did marry their daughter in Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- or was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. OK. Please identify this baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the grandchild of the people that care of Alice during the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the picture of my son Michael during his bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please identify these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of Michael-- my son, Michael, at the wedding in 19-- 60-- [INAUDIBLE] 19-- 90-- 3-- 1993 or '92. It was three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a wedding now--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What does he do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. He's a-- he's a professor in the university in New Jersey. A very good boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please identify the person in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of Martin, my oldest boy. He's a speech therapist in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please identify this baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the baby of Aviva's-- this is Aviva's grandchild. And Aviva is the daughter of my sister's sister-- of my wife's sister, which is in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about this book?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the book written by my friend, Alexander Donat, which was together starting from Warsaw to the end of the war. We were all in the same camps together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please introduce the lovely lady sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Julia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 60 Years. Over 60 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your anniversary?

speaker: interviewee
text: June 6th.

speaker: interviewee
text: July 6th.

speaker: interviewee
text: July 6th. I mix them up a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And great-grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very lovely meeting you, and thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. We are expecting a new grandson in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Mazel tov ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know if it's a grandson. Grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's wonderful. Congratulations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
